title: Visualizing Top NBA Performers in Python
text:
When every player is elite, it can be difficult to tell who is truly standing out among the stars. Traditional box score stats like points or rebounds only tell part of the story. Using advanced analytics allows us to quantify overall contributions, efficiency, and impact, helping identify players who might be undervalued or underacknowledged by casual fans.
In this tutorial, you’ll learn how to load NBA data from publicly available sources and use Python to explore these advanced metrics. The dataset we’ll use contains advanced stats for major players in the current NBA season (2025–2026) and is available at Basketball Reference. By the end of this guide, you’ll be able to filter, visualize, and compare top performers using meaningful metrics.

title: Visualizing Top NBA Performers in Python
text:
When every player is elite, it can be difficult to tell who is truly standing out among the stars. Traditional box score stats like points or rebounds only tell part of the story. Using advanced analytics allows us to quantify overall contributions, efficiency, and impact, helping identify players who might be undervalued or underacknowledged by casual fans.
In this tutorial, you’ll learn how to load NBA data from publicly available sources and use Python to explore these advanced metrics. The dataset we’ll use contains advanced stats for major players in the current NBA season (2025–2026) and is available at Basketball Reference. By the end of this guide, you’ll be able to filter, visualize, and compare top performers using meaningful metrics.

title: Visualizing Top NBA Performers in Python
text:
Loading in the Data
We first want to import the libraries we’ll be using for this, as well as the data. For this project we’ll be using pandas and matplotlib. These will allow for us to handle the data and eventually visualize it. There are also sometimes multiple tables on the site, so using table[0] selects the first one.


Code
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

url = "https://www.basketball-reference.com/leagues/NBA_2026_advanced.html"
table = pd.read_html(url)
# Selects table with all the data we want
players = table[0]


Let’s look at what our data looks like without any filtering.

title: Visualizing Top NBA Performers in Python
text:
Filtering the Data
The data is already in a really nice format so the majority of our filtering will just be to focus on a subset of players rather than doing any cleaning of data.
There is one common issue when using Basketball Reference. It’s that the Rk tab at the top of the table actually repeats after about 20 or 30 cells. We want to get rid of that so we don’t have any issues with it farther down the line.


Code
players = players[players["Rk"] != "Rk"]


Now we want to filter the data to the best of the best players, the All-Stars for this NBA season. All-Stars in the NBA are selected by a combination of players and fans voting on who they think the best players in the NBA are currently. To do this I just created a list with every All-Star and then filtered only for NBA players that are within that list.


Code
all_stars = ["Scottie Barnes","Devin Booker","Cade Cunningham", "Jalen Duren","Anthony Edwards",
             "Chet Holmgren","Jalen Johnson","Tyrese Maxey","Jaylen Brown","Jalen Brunson",
             "Kevin Durant","De'Aaron Fox","Brandon Ingram", "Lebron James","Kawhi Leonard",
             "Donovan Mitchell","Stephen Curry","Deni Avdija","Luka Dončić",
             "Shai Gilgeous-Alexander","Nikola Jokić", "Jamal Murray","Norman Powell",
             "Alperen Sengun","Pascsal Siakam","Karl-Anthony Towns","Victor Wembanyama",
             "Giannis Antetokounmpo"]

# Filters DataFrame to just All-Star players
all_star_players = players[players["Player"].isin(all_stars)]

title: Visualizing Top NBA Performers in Python
text:
Plotting
As I referenced earlier I will be using matplotlib for this portion. The advanced stat of my choosing is going to be PER (Player Efficiency Rating). PER is a formula that combines all the good and bad things a player does into a single number. It’s a good way to get a sense of how all-around elite a player is.


Code
# Ensures that PER is treated as a numeric type
all_star_players["PER"] = pd.to_numeric(all_star_players["PER"], errors="coerce")

# Make the plot BIG
plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))

# Plots players as bars
plt.bar(all_star_players["Player"], all_star_players["PER"], color="skyblue")

# Makes x-axis labels readable
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
plt.ylabel("PER")
plt.title("2025-2026 NBA All-Stars - PER Comparison")
plt.tight_layout()

# Save figure for embedding
plt.savefig("all_stars_per.png")

title: Visualizing Top NBA Performers in Python
text:
Conclusion
We now have a beautiful plot! We can see that the three standout players are Nikola Jokic, Shai Gilgeous-Alexander, and Giannis Antetokounmpo, which reflects the validity of this advanced stat as all three of those players are considered the most valuable to their team. It’s worth mentioning that the league average for PER is about 15, and every single person in this set is above that number.
You can now use this info to do all sorts of player comparisons for yourself or look into differences in team stats as that data is also available on Basketball Reference!

title: Data Acquisition Project
text: Have you ever wanted to see your Spotify wrapped before they release it to everyone, or wanted to access the data for your favorite songs in a much more efficient way? Fortunately, Spotify’s API lets you do both, quickly and efficiently. In this blog post I’ll show a brief tutorial on how you can get started yourself and an example of how I found out if my songs rank was correlated by the duration of the song.

title: Data Acquisition Project
text: Have you ever wanted to see your Spotify wrapped before they release it to everyone, or wanted to access the data for your favorite songs in a much more efficient way? Fortunately, Spotify’s API lets you do both, quickly and efficiently. In this blog post I’ll show a brief tutorial on how you can get started yourself and an example of how I found out if my songs rank was correlated by the duration of the song.

title: Data Acquisition Project
text:
Getting Credentials
Before accessing Spotify’s data you need to register a developer application to get your API credentials. This can be done by visiting Spotify for Developers and logging in. It’s worth noting that this does require a Spotify Premium account. Once you create your app you will need to copy and paste your Client ID and Client Secret, as well as set a Redirect URL. All three of these things will be required for proper authentication. I recommend storing these in a .env file, allowing you to keep the keys out of your code like below.


Code
CLIENT_ID=your_client_id_here
CLIENT_SECRET=your_client_secret_here
REDIRECT_URI= http://localhost:8888/callback

title: Data Acquisition Project
text:
Authenticating with Spotipy
Spotipy is a wrapper for Spotify Web API, which simplifies authentication and data retrieval. The documentation for it can be found here. It also ensures that you only access data that you are allowed to see and you don’t perform anything unethical via spotipy.oauth2. All the imports and the basic authentication are shown below. The Scope parameter gives permission to obtain the user’s top songs over different time periods. (short_term - 4 weeks, medium_term - 6 months, and long_term - all-time)


Code
import spotipy
from spotipy.oauth2 import SpotifyOAuth
from dotenv import load_dotenv
import os
import pandas as pd
import csv

load_dotenv()

sp = spotipy.Spotify(auth_manager=SpotifyOAuth(
    client_id=os.getenv("CLIENT_ID"),
    client_secret=os.getenv("CLIENT_SECRET"),
    redirect_uri=os.getenv("REDIRECT_URI"),
    scope="user-top-read"
))

title: Data Acquisition Project
text:
Acquiring the Top 200 Tracks
Spotify’s API allows a maximum of 50 tracks per request, so to get around this you need to re-request after the first request for 50 has gone through. We do this by using a step of 50 with offset being the index and then extending the list.


Code
top_tracks = []
for offset in range(0, 200, 50):
    results = sp.current_user_top_tracks(limit=50, offset=offset, time_range='long_term')
    top_tracks.extend(results['items'])


Each track has the columns name, artists, album, duration_ms, and id.

title: Data Acquisition Project
section: Saving Our Data to a CSV
text:
Saving Our Data to a CSV
We now simply write our data to a CSV, converting the duration_ms into seconds making it easier to work with in the future.


Code
with open("top_200_tracks.csv", "w", newline="", encoding="utf-8") as csvfile:
    writer = csv.writer(csvfile)
    writer.writerow(["Rank", "Track Name", "Artists", "Album", "Duration_seconds", "Track ID"])

    for idx, track in enumerate(top_tracks, start=1):
        artists = ', '.join([a['name'] for a in track['artists']])
        duration_seconds = round(track['duration_ms'] / 1000, 2)
        writer.writerow([idx, track['name'], artists, track['album']['name'], duration_seconds, track['id']])

title: Data Acquisition Project
text:
Correlation Between Rank and Duration
Pandas makes exploring correlations easy, and for this analysis I’ll be using a Pearson correlation which measures linear relationships. This will tell us if as duration increases does the rank of the track also increase or vice versa. A result of 1 or -1 indicates strong correlation while a result near 0 indicates no correlation.


Code
top_200 = pd.read_csv("top_200_tracks.csv")
pearson_corr = top_200['Rank'].corr(top_200['Duration_seconds'], method='pearson')


Doing this we get the correlation of -0.0001 which means there is no linear relationship between rank and duration.

title: Data Acquisition Project
text:
Conclusion
With just a few Python packages, you can explore your Spotify listening habits in depth. Spotipy’s OAuth authentication makes it simple to access personal data, while CSV export and pandas analysis allow you to measure correlations. From converting milliseconds to seconds to calculating Pearson correlations, you now have a complete workflow for exploring whether track duration affects your personal song rankings or exploring any other questions that your heart desires.

title: About Me
text: Nathan Palmer

title: About Me
text:
Background
I am a junior at BYU studying Statistics with an emphasis in Data Science! I’m interested in finding patterns in data and using them to make data driven decisions.
Some of my previous coursework projects include:

Bayesian Inference for Competitive Balance (Oct–Dec 2025): Built a Beta-Binomial hierarchical model to estimate NBA conference win probabilities; processed 200+ game records and quantified uncertainty with Monte Carlo simulations.

Comparative Player Performance Modeling (Mar–Apr 2025): Scraped and cleaned NBA game logs, created visual analytics, and developed matched-opponent comparisons to fairly evaluate MVP candidates.

Mixed-Effects ANOVA for HCI Study (Oct–Dec 2024): Designed and analyzed a typing-speed experiment with mixed-effects models; led team in data collection, diagnostics, and presentation.

In addition to my academic work, I completed my Eagle Scout project, where I built a garden plot and benches for a Civil War museum, leading the planning, construction, and coordination with museum staff.

Get To Know Me
I’m a passionate sports fan, cheering for the Cleveland Browns**, Indiana Pacers, and BYU. I enjoy staying active through playing basketball and running. I love board games and trivia and was on the trivia team in high school when I memorized every capital of every country in the world (I still remember most of them). I also love the outdoors, I camp, hike, and fish with my brother often.

text:

In the short term I want to better integrate machine learning into my skill set and gain more practical experience with cleaning data and visualization. In the future I hope to apply analytics to improve player performance, strategy, and team decision making on a major sports team.
